source: GolfWRX premium vs value thread, TheSandTrap ball loss thread

quotes.quote: My conclusion is that once you get to be a good enough player where you lose few, if any, golf balls per round, it is worth just paying up to use the best ball you can afford every time.
I'm a 7 handicap and probably average 1 or 2 lost balls per round, depending on the course. I feel like the few bucks I'm saving on a value golf ball don't matter given the cost of the rounds.

If you dont lose balls, its worth paying for. I retire more balls than I lose. And when you get comfortable with a certain ball, and know how it feels, know how it spins, how it checks up, it really does help your game.

I previously played Kirkland, but after the MGS test figured that I should move more to a 'middle of the road' performance ball rather than something that's a severe outlier for spin and lack of distance.

I bought 60 MTB-X for ~$170 w/shipping and tax. That'd cost $250 for ProV1. I don't expect to lose 60 balls in a year, and if I do, am I really sweating $80 difference? Nah. So what, my ball budget is $6.66 per month different between the two?

My suggestion would be to play the ball you like. If it's the Snell, good for you, you'll save a little coin. If it's the TP5x or ProV1, well, you'll spend a little more. But IMHO your preference is worth it, whatever that is.
But when I was a beginner losing 3, 4, 5 balls on a bad day my limit was $1 a ball.
If you routinely play two or more rounds with the same ball, it literally does not matter how much it cost. Even a $5 ball every other round is trivial compared to the other costs in golf.

From one San Diegan to another, I'll just tell you it helps immensely. Learning one ball helps immensely. I bought 5 dozen TP5x practice balls on sale and I decided to try a dozen Prov1 about 3-4 dozen deep. I had no feel.
I was leaving approaches short or long, wasn't grabbing greens, rolling out on chips etc. It just helps to be consistent.
After reading the review of the new prov1 from MGS and how it is virtually defect free and reading about how scuffed balls can cost distance and accuracy, I switched to using only new yellow prov1s.
I don't like paying $53.50/doz either. But your cost analysis is correct. Pay for a new ball unless you can't afford it.

Personally I think it's only worth buying Titleist because their manufacturing process is apparently the best. I mean you're using a new ball because you want it to be perfect. What's the point of using a new ball if it's a manufacturing defect?

I seem to keep going back to the Kirkland in my rotation because I like the way the ball feels and performs and I score well with it. After the MGS test came out I started questioning whether I should still play the ball.
I played 2-3 rounds with it just to confirm the MGS tests in my game. Well, waddaya know, I played really well with them and still liked the feel and performance so the K-sig is in my rotation.
I have so many golf balls of varying brands (about 14 dozen) that I sometimes stand on deck at the 1st tee not knowing which ball to play.

The MGS test showed that the Kirkland was a severe statistical outlier compared to other urethane balls. Maybe it's great to have those spin numbers, but honestly I play public courses with receptive greens.
Why would I voluntarily give away distance for extra spin I don't need?
Cost: While $25/2doz is a SMOKING deal, and I absolutely love Costco, I honestly don't lose enough balls that I care.
Color: I prefer yellow; I see them better in the sky and they're easier to find in the rough. Snell offers a BRIGHT freakin' yellow color.

I tried them and I lost 1 club from my irons and about 10 yards or more with the driver. I verified this on the sim indoors and in real life game situations. As much as I want to play Kirkland because I am cheap, I don't.
While urethane balls are nice, no doubt, I do not have any issues stopping a Srixon Soft Feel or Callaway Supersoft using my wedges or short or mid irons.
If I play each ball twice, here is my projected cost per ball: $2.75 - Premium, $1.8 - Mid Tier, $1.2 - Entry Level. If I look at it this way, it does not hurt much.
I recently fell into the Callaway Supersoft by accident. I accidentally pulled out a SuperSoft Max that I'd found out of my bag on the first tee and played pretty well with it.
I shot two of my best rounds in the last 12 months with the SuperSoft. It's long off the tee, flies a predictable distance with wedges and is fine for my short game.
I lose a lot of balls and still play premium (TP5x and Left Dash). It's a feel thing more than anything. The game wouldn't feel as fun.
I find more clean pro v1s on my course than I lose. I don't lose balls often and retire them for scuffs due to sand etc more often.

I average one or two lost balls per week, which is four or five rounds, and I still won't pay premium prices for balls. I would if I scored any better with them, but I don't, so it's Callaway Supersoft for me.
I stocked up on new TM TP/X 'practice' balls for $1.25 per ball and will have enough for the next 5 years. I put a putting line on my ball anyway and it goes nicely over the 'practice' part.
I would say that normally I will lose 1 or 2 golf balls every time I play 18 holes. I've played several rounds with only 1 ball.
Of course we've all had those days where you lose a half dozen or more balls in one round. Or even one of those holes where you lose 3 balls on one hole.
Fall is coming. When the course is covered in leaves we 'lose' balls all the time: right down the middle...never see it again.
I couldn't vote. I lose on average, 1 ball every 6 rounds. I am guessing that number even at that.
I don't often lose a golf ball due to the fact that I am a short hitter and almost always hit the fairway. There are holes where it is easy to lose a ball, but I always steer away from those areas.
This is highly dependent. I can lose as few as 0 or as many as 7, but on the average round it's usually 1-2 these days. Which is a pretty good improvement.
I would say 1-2 is typical, and I can usually find a couple - depending on the course. So I don't carry that many, since 'found' balls can be used as an emergency replacement.

I'll have an occasional round where I will lose more, sometimes these include the unexplainable alien abductions like my first tee shot of the year which was fairly well struck to the right side middle of the fairway and was never found.
The funny thing is I probably lose more good and new balls than old ones.
More often, I take one out of play due to scuffs from the cartpath.

I used to lose a lot of balls. Couple of sleeves wasn't impossible. Now my worst days I lose 3. Usually 1-2. But for the first time ever this year I have played multiple rounds where I have also lost none!
I use a ball for a couple rounds then it goes into my shag bag. After a couple years in the shag bag into the garbage they go.
There really isn't a normal... 0 more often than not, but it can easily become 3 or more in the blink of an eye depending on the day and course.

After losing half a dozen balls in less than nine holes I figured a good compromise was to buy a hundred used golf balls. Same ball all the time to simplify my game. No worries about the cost of the balls since I got them at a really good price.
The only thing I really dislike is too much spin 100yds in and around the greens. Other than that it's a very good ball for the price.
I'm really playing 2 balls which I feel is very interchangeable, Snell Black and Maxfli Tour. Those balls are so close you could play either and never tell difference.
I typically buy 'tour' balls at year end when they are discounted, or Vice golf balls which you always get a good price on if you buy like 4-5 pack.
Right now I have 5 boxes of 2020 Zstars and 4 boxes of 2021 Vice Pros.
If you're a '7' while losing 1 or 2 balls per round, you can PLAY. You might need a little help with course management though.

Even chipping and putting similar balls can exhibit slight differences. Take a relatively short chip shot. If you deliver the club consistently you'll probably notice a little bit more 'bite' with one ball over another.
Also, it's likely one ball will 'feel' better off the putter or off the wedge when you're chipping. Feel matters too.
